content.text: The RAG Evaluation Framework Landscape

Several frameworks have emerged to standardize RAG evaluation. RAGAS is the most widely adopted for offline batch evaluation, computing faithfulness, answer relevancy, context precision, and context recall out of the box. DeepEval integrates with pytest for CI-native threshold gates. TruLens and LangSmith focus on production tracing — they instrument your running pipeline and surface evaluation metrics alongside traces in a dashboard. Understanding when to reach for each framework prevents reinventing evaluation infrastructure.

attrs.title: RAGAS, DeepEval, TruLens, and LangSmith — when to use each

content.text: RAGAS: Offline Batch Evaluation

RAGAS (Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment) takes a HuggingFace Dataset with question, answer, contexts, and optionally ground_truth columns, then computes a battery of metrics using an LLM judge. It is the standard starting point for RAG evaluation because it covers both the retrieval and generation stages without requiring hand-labeled relevance judgments for the core metrics.

# pip install ragas datasets
from datasets import Dataset
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import (
    faithfulness,
    answer_relevancy,
    context_precision,
    context_recall,
    answer_correctness,
)
from ragas.llms import LangchainLLMWrapper
from ragas.embeddings import LangchainEmbeddingsWrapper
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings

# Configure which LLM and embeddings RAGAS uses internally for judging
ragas_llm = LangchainLLMWrapper(ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0))
ragas_emb  = LangchainEmbeddingsWrapper(OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"))

# Evaluation dataset — build this by running your RAG pipeline on test questions
data = {
    "question":     ["What is the return window?", "How do I reset my password?"],
    "answer":       ["30 days with original packaging.", "Click Forgot Password on login."],
    "contexts":     [
        ["Electronics: 30-day return. Original packaging required."],
        ["Reset: click Forgot Password, check email, follow link within 24h."],
    ],
    "ground_truth": ["30-day return window.", "Use the Forgot Password link."],
}
dataset = Dataset.from_dict(data)

results = evaluate(
    dataset,
    metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall, answer_correctness],
    llm=ragas_llm,
    embeddings=ragas_emb,
)

df = results.to_pandas()
print(df[["faithfulness", "answer_relevancy", "context_precision",
          "context_recall", "answer_correctness"]].mean().round(3))
DeepEval: pytest-Native CI Evaluation

DeepEval turns RAG evaluation into pytest test cases with assert-style threshold gates. Each metric is a Python object with a threshold; the test passes or fails based on the score. This makes it easy to wire evaluation into CI — a failing test blocks a merge just like a failing unit test would.

# pip install deepeval
# test_rag_eval.py — run with: pytest test_rag_eval.py
import pytest
from deepeval import assert_test
from deepeval.metrics import (
    FaithfulnessMetric,
    AnswerRelevancyMetric,
    ContextualPrecisionMetric,
    HallucinationMetric,
)
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase

# Define metrics with thresholds — test fails if score < threshold
faithfulness_metric  = FaithfulnessMetric(threshold=0.85)
relevancy_metric     = AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.80)
hallucination_metric = HallucinationMetric(threshold=0.20)  # LOWER is better for hallucination


@pytest.mark.parametrize("question,answer,context,ground_truth", [
    (
        "What is the return window for electronics?",
        "Electronics can be returned within 30 days with original packaging.",
        ["Electronics: 30-day return window. Original packaging required."],
        "30-day return window for electronics.",
    ),
    (
        "How do I reset my password?",
        "Click Forgot Password on the login page.",
        ["Reset password: click Forgot Password, follow email link within 24h."],
        "Use the Forgot Password link on the login page.",
    ),
])
def test_rag_answer(question, answer, context, ground_truth):
    test_case = LLMTestCase(
        input=question,
        actual_output=answer,
        retrieval_context=context,
        expected_output=ground_truth,
    )
    assert_test(test_case, [faithfulness_metric, relevancy_metric, hallucination_metric])
TruLens: Production Tracing with the RAG Triad

TruLens instruments your RAG pipeline as a decorator and records every input, retrieval result, and output to a local database. It evaluates each run against the RAG Triad — context relevance, groundedness, and answer relevance — and surfaces results in a web dashboard. The key advantage over RAGAS is that TruLens evaluates live production calls, not just offline batches.

# pip install trulens-eval
from trulens_eval import Tru, TruChain, Feedback
from trulens_eval.feedback.provider import OpenAI as TruOpenAI

tru = Tru()  # Starts local SQLite tracking

provider = TruOpenAI(model_engine="gpt-4o-mini")

# Define the three RAG Triad metrics
context_relevance = (
    Feedback(provider.context_relevance_with_cot_reasons, name="Context Relevance")
    .on_input()
    .on(TruChain.select_context())
    .aggregate(min)  # Use min across passages — weakest link matters
)

groundedness = (
    Feedback(provider.groundedness_measure_with_cot_reasons, name="Groundedness")
    .on(TruChain.select_context().collect())
    .on_output()
)

answer_relevance = (
    Feedback(provider.relevance_with_cot_reasons, name="Answer Relevance")
    .on_input_output()
)

feedbacks = [context_relevance, groundedness, answer_relevance]

# Wrap your LangChain RAG chain
tru_chain = TruChain(
    rag_chain,                      # your existing LangChain chain
    app_id="policy-rag-v1",
    feedbacks=feedbacks,
)

# Every call through tru_chain is now evaluated and logged
with tru_chain as recording:
    answer = tru_chain.invoke("What is the return window for electronics?")

# View results in dashboard: tru.run_dashboard()
records, feedback_df = tru.get_records_and_feedback(app_ids=["policy-rag-v1"])
print(feedback_df[["Context Relevance", "Groundedness", "Answer Relevance"]].mean())
LangSmith: Tracing and Evaluation for LangChain Pipelines

LangSmith is LangChain's native evaluation and observability platform. It traces every chain and agent run automatically when LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2 is set, and provides a UI for inspecting individual steps, running evaluators on logged traces, and comparing prompt versions. It is the most seamless choice for teams already using LangChain.

import os
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2"]  = "true"
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_API_KEY"]     = "your-langsmith-api-key"
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_PROJECT"]     = "policy-rag"

# That's it — all LangChain calls are now traced automatically
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Answer using only this context."),
    ("human", "Context: {context}\nQuestion: {question}"),
]) | llm | StrOutputParser()

answer = chain.invoke({"context": "30-day return.", "question": "What is the return window?"})
# Full trace visible in LangSmith dashboard at smith.langchain.com

# Run programmatic evaluators on a dataset
from langsmith import Client
from langsmith.evaluation import evaluate as ls_evaluate

client = Client()

def faithfulness_evaluator(run, example) -> dict:
    """Custom evaluator function for LangSmith."""
    result = check_faithfulness(run.outputs["output"], example.inputs["context"])
    return {"score": result.score, "key": "faithfulness"}

ls_evaluate(
    chain.invoke,
    data="policy-rag-eval-set",     # dataset name in LangSmith
    evaluators=[faithfulness_evaluator],
    experiment_prefix="gpt4o-chunk512",
)

Synthetic Test Set Generation with RAGAS

Building a ground-truth evaluation set by hand is time-consuming. RAGAS provides a TestsetGenerator that takes your document corpus and automatically creates (question, context, ground_truth) triples using an LLM. This is an effective way to bootstrap an evaluation set for a new domain before you have real user queries to label.

from ragas.testset.generator import TestsetGenerator
from ragas.testset.evolutions import simple, reasoning, multi_context
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.document_loaders import DirectoryLoader

# Load your document corpus
loader = DirectoryLoader("./docs", glob="**/*.md")
documents = loader.load()

# Configure the generator
generator_llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
critic_llm    = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")  # Stronger model for quality filtering
embeddings    = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

generator = TestsetGenerator.from_langchain(generator_llm, critic_llm, embeddings)

# Generate 50 test cases with a mix of question types
testset = generator.generate_with_langchain_docs(
    documents,
    test_size=50,
    distributions={
        simple:        0.5,   # Simple factual questions
        reasoning:     0.3,   # Multi-step reasoning required
        multi_context: 0.2,   # Requires combining multiple passages
    },
)

# Export for review and manual correction
df = testset.to_pandas()
df.to_csv("eval_data/synthetic_testset.csv", index=False)
print(f"Generated {len(df)} test cases")
print(df[["question", "ground_truth", "evolution_type"]].head(3))
Integrating RAGAS Into a CI Pipeline

Wiring RAGAS into CI ensures every change to the RAG pipeline — new embedding model, different chunking, updated prompt — is measured against the same benchmark before merging. The pattern: run the RAG pipeline on the evaluation set, compute RAGAS metrics, assert thresholds, and write results to a JSON artifact for trend tracking.

# ci_eval.py
import sys
import json
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
from datasets import Dataset
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall

EVAL_CSV   = Path("eval_data/synthetic_testset.csv")
RESULTS_DIR = Path("eval_results")
RESULTS_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

THRESHOLDS = {
    "faithfulness":       0.85,
    "answer_relevancy":   0.80,
    "context_precision":  0.75,
    "context_recall":     0.70,
}


def run_pipeline_on_eval_set(df: pd.DataFrame) -> list[dict]:
    """Run your RAG pipeline on each question. Replace stub with real chain."""
    results = []
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        answer, contexts = your_rag_chain(row["question"])
        results.append({
            "question":     row["question"],
            "answer":       answer,
            "contexts":     contexts,
            "ground_truth": row["ground_truth"],
        })
    return results


def main(run_label: str = "ci"):
    df = pd.read_csv(EVAL_CSV)
    rows = run_pipeline_on_eval_set(df)

    dataset = Dataset.from_list(rows)
    results = evaluate(
        dataset,
        metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall],
    )
    scores = results.to_pandas()[list(THRESHOLDS)].mean().to_dict()

    # Persist results for trend tracking
    output = {"run": run_label, **{k: round(v, 4) for k, v in scores.items()}}
    out_path = RESULTS_DIR / f"{run_label}.json"
    out_path.write_text(json.dumps(output, indent=2))

    # Gate on thresholds
    failures = [f"{k}={scores[k]:.3f} < {t}" for k, t in THRESHOLDS.items() if scores[k] < t]
    if failures:
        print("EVAL FAILED:", "; ".join(failures))
        sys.exit(1)
    print("EVAL PASSED:", {k: f"{v:.3f}" for k, v in scores.items()})


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    p = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    p.add_argument("--run-label", default="ci")
    main(p.parse_args().run_label)
Choosing an LLM Judge for Evaluation

All three frameworks use an LLM to judge metric scores. The choice of judge model significantly affects both cost and reliability. A stronger judge (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet) produces more consistent verdicts but costs 10–20× more per eval call than a smaller model. For most teams the right balance is: use GPT-4o-mini for faithfulness and context precision (binary classification tasks), and GPT-4o only for answer correctness and G-Eval (which require nuanced semantic judgment).

from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, answer_correctness
from ragas.llms import LangchainLLMWrapper
from ragas.embeddings import LangchainEmbeddingsWrapper
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings

# Cheaper judge for high-volume binary metrics
cheap_llm = LangchainLLMWrapper(ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0))
cheap_emb = LangchainEmbeddingsWrapper(OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"))

# Stronger judge for nuanced metrics only
strong_llm = LangchainLLMWrapper(ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0))

# Assign judge per metric
faithfulness.llm         = cheap_llm    # binary classification → cheap judge fine
answer_relevancy.llm     = cheap_llm
answer_relevancy.embeddings = cheap_emb
answer_correctness.llm   = strong_llm  # semantic similarity → stronger judge needed

from datasets import Dataset
from ragas import evaluate

results = evaluate(
    dataset,
    metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, answer_correctness],
    # Each metric uses its own assigned llm — no global override needed
)
print(results)
# Approximate cost reduction: ~60% vs using gpt-4o for all metrics
Summary
RAGAS is the default choice for offline batch evaluation — it covers retrieval and generation metrics without requiring ground-truth labels for the core metrics.
DeepEval integrates with pytest, turning evaluation into test cases with explicit threshold gates that block CI on regression.
TruLens instruments live pipelines via a decorator and evaluates the RAG Triad (context relevance, groundedness, answer relevance) in production.
LangSmith is the most seamless choice for LangChain users — auto-tracing with LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true and dataset-based evaluators.
Use RAGAS or DeepEval for development/CI and TruLens or LangSmith for production monitoring — the two modes complement each other.
